title: AI Agents for Law Firms in Jacksonville, FL: 2026 Implementation Guide
h1: AI Agents for Law Firms in Jacksonville, FL: The 2026 Implementation Guide
metaDescription: Complete 2026 guide to deploying AI agents at Jacksonville law firms. Claude-powered intake, conflict checks, Florida Bar Opinion 24-1 compliance, ROI math, 90-day plan.
primaryKeyword: AI agents for law firms in Jacksonville
tldr: Jacksonville is the fastest-growing major city in Florida (1,032,061 residents, +1.41%/yr) and one of the most competitive legal markets in the Southeast - dominated by Morgan & Morgan, Pajcic & Pajcic, and Florin|Roebig but also home to hundreds of 1-15 attorney firms losing intake to faster competitors. This guide shows how to deploy a Claude-powered three-agent stack (intake, conflict-check, document review) under Florida Bar Opinion 24-1 in 90 days, with ROI math and concrete deployment steps.
quickFacts: Florida has **over 113,000 licensed attorneys** through The Florida Bar - the third-largest legal market in the U.S. (The Florida Bar, 2025).
Jacksonville is the **fastest-growing major city in Florida** with a 2026 population of **1,032,061** and 1.41% annual growth (World Population Review, 2026).

**12.6% of Jacksonville residents are Hispanic** and the Hispanic population grew **51% between 2010 and 2020** - meaning Spanish-speaking intake is now a real competitive advantage for Duval firms (U.S. Census).
Florida Bar **Opinion 24-1 (Jan 19, 2024)** affirmatively permits generative AI in law practice with four conditions: confidentiality, competence, fair billing, and clear chatbot disclosure.

Average Jacksonville **legal PPC cost per click ranges $50-$200** for personal-injury and family-law keywords, with cost per signed case often above **$2,500** - which is why missing a single after-hours lead is expensive.

Two major Naval installations - **NAS Jacksonville and Naval Station Mayport** - support 40,000+ military personnel and families, generating sustained demand for family law, estate planning, and military-specific civil practice.
**Jacksonville homeowners insurance has risen ~226%** in five years to roughly $4,200/year - fueling an active insurance-bad-faith and property-claim litigation segment unique to Florida.

sections.heading: Why Jacksonville law firms need AI agents in 2026
sections.body:
Jacksonville is a strange and very specific legal market. It is geographically the largest city in the contiguous United States by land area, it grew its Hispanic population by **51% between 2010 and 2020**, and it sits inside a state whose Bar approved generative AI for legal work in [Opinion 24-1](https://www.floridabar.org/etopinions/opinion-24-1/) more than two years ago. The combination of those three facts - massive geography, fast-shifting demographics, and a green-lit ethics framework - is exactly why AI agents are no longer optional for Duval County firms in 2026.

Three pressures define the market right now:

**First, the dominant firms are spending you out of organic search.** Morgan & Morgan, headquartered in Florida, recently won a $20 million Jacksonville verdict and runs national-scale advertising. Pajcic & Pajcic has been in Jacksonville since 1974 and posts $1.5 billion in lifetime recoveries. Florin|Roebig has $1 billion in client results. If you are a 3-attorney boutique competing against those firms on Google Ads, your cost-per-signed-case is brutal - the only place you can out-execute them is on speed-to-lead and intake conversion. Both are exactly what AI fixes.

**Second, the Florida Bar has explicitly said this is allowed.** Unlike states where ethics counsel is still hedging, Florida Bar Opinion 24-1 unanimously approved generative AI use in law practice in January 2024, subject to four guardrails: client confidentiality, competence/accuracy, fair billing, and clear chatbot disclosure. Firms that wait for more clarity are waiting for clarity that already arrived.

**Third, Jacksonville's demographic mix rewards 24/7 multilingual intake.** With 12.6% Hispanic residents (and growing fast), large Puerto Rican and Cuban populations, and a 40,000+ military community across NAS Jacksonville and Naval Station Mayport, the firms that can answer a Spanish-language intake question at 11pm on a Sunday - or a deployment-affected family-law question at 4am - are winning matters their competitors never even see. Claude is natively multilingual and never sleeps.

At manuv.co we see the pattern weekly with Jacksonville-area firms: the question is no longer *whether* to deploy AI, it is *which three agents* to deploy first under Opinion 24-1, and how to do it without bumping into Rule 4-7.13.

sections.heading: The three-agent stack for Jacksonville firms
sections.body:
The biggest mistake we see Jacksonville firms make is shopping the legal-AI-vendor universe looking for a single platform that does everything. They get overwhelmed by the SaaS noise, sign nothing, and lose another year of compounding advantage to Morgan & Morgan's national infrastructure.

The pattern that actually deploys is a three-agent stack, each addressing a distinct bottleneck in the small-and-mid Duval firm workflow:

**Agent 1 - The intake agent.** A Claude-powered web/SMS/phone agent that captures and pre-qualifies leads 24/7, books consultations on the firm's calendar, and writes structured intake notes into Clio, MyCase, or PracticePanther. For most Jacksonville firms this is the single highest-ROI deployment - the place where current revenue is leaking the fastest.

**Agent 2 - The Claude Code conflict-check skill.** A packaged automation that runs fuzzy-matched conflict searches against the firm's matter database in seconds rather than days, with an audit log that satisfies Rule 4-1.7 documentation expectations.

**Agent 3 - The document review agent.** A retrieval-grounded Claude agent that summarizes deposition transcripts, redlines contracts, and screens discovery production. Replaces 60-80% of the associate hours currently being burned on review work at $250-$400/hour.

Each agent ships in 2-4 weeks. Together they cover the three exact places small-and-mid Jacksonville firms hemorrhage value: leads that don't get answered before 9am Monday, new matters that take days to clear conflicts, and associate hours burning on review tasks that Claude does for under a dollar of API spend.

sections.heading: Agent 1: Claude-powered intake
sections.body:
Intake is where almost every Jacksonville firm we audit is bleeding the most money. Personal-injury, family-law, immigration, criminal-defense - all four practice areas live or die on first-contact response time, and we routinely find Duval firms whose average response is **over four hours** for after-hours web-form leads. Meanwhile [Lex Reception data](https://www.lexreception.com/) and other industry studies show legal leads answered within 5 minutes book consultations roughly 21x more often than leads that wait over 30.

A realistic Jacksonville intake flow looks like this. A driver gets rear-ended on I-95 near Southside Boulevard at 11:42pm. They Google "Jacksonville car accident lawyer" from the side of the road. Three firms get clicked. Two send a web-form auto-reply promising callback in 24 hours. The third - the one running a Claude Sonnet 4.5 intake agent - texts back within 90 seconds in the driver's language (Spanish or English), asks for crash basics (date, location, injuries, medical care, insurance), pre-checks the at-fault driver's name against the firm's matter database for conflicts, books a 9:30am consultation, and drops a structured intake note into Clio Grow. By the time the partner has her coffee at 9:00am, she sees a qualified, conflict-cleared, Spanish-bilingual case with a scheduled call. The other two firms are leaving voicemails.

### What makes a Florida intake agent compliant

Florida Bar Opinion 24-1 is explicit about chatbot-and-intake usage. Three requirements matter most:

- **Disclosure that the user is talking to AI.** The agent must identify itself as an AI program, not a lawyer or staff member, in the first message. We hard-code this into the system prompt and into the opening web-chat greeting.
- **No practice of law.** The agent is restricted to triage and scheduling. It does not negotiate, evaluate case value, or give legal advice. The system prompt explicitly refuses any prompt asking for legal opinion.
- **Screening for represented persons.** Opinion 24-1 recommends screening questions that limit the chatbot's communications if the prospective client is already represented. We include an early-flow check: "Are you currently represented by another attorney for this matter?" - and route accordingly.

Underlying infrastructure: Anthropic's commercial Claude API (not Claude.ai consumer), with a Data Processing Addendum and (where applicable) a Business Associate Agreement covering the [HIPAA-eligible Messages API path](https://privacy.claude.com/en/articles/8114513-business-associate-agreements-baa-for-commercial-customers). Transcripts route to the firm's matter management system, not to a third-party vendor's data warehouse.

Typical deployment at a 6-attorney Jacksonville firm: 2 weeks. Monthly ongoing cost (Claude API + hosting + SMS gateway): roughly $400-$700. Replaces $3,500-$5,000/month of after-hours answering service and paralegal-monitored chat - and captures revenue that previously went to whoever woke up first.

sections.heading: Agent 2: Claude Code conflict-check skill
sections.body:
Conflict checks are the silent killer of mid-size Duval County firms. Florida Rule 4-1.7 requires careful conflict analysis on every new matter, and most Jacksonville firms still do this manually - a paralegal queries the matter database, the partner reviews, weeks go by, and an opposing counsel calls before the engagement letter is signed.

Claude Code skills are the right deployment unit. A Claude Code skill is a packaged, versioned automation that runs locally - against the firm's actual data, not in a cloud sandbox - and produces structured, auditable output. Ethics counsel loves it because it leaves a paper trail; partners love it because it finishes in seconds.

### What the skill does

1. Takes new-matter inputs: client name, opposing parties, related corporate entities, witness list, prior counsel.
2. Runs fuzzy-match queries against the firm's matter database - whether that's Clio Manage, MyCase, PracticePanther, Smokeball, Filevine, NetDocuments, or a legacy on-premises SQL Server.
3. Flags exact matches, near matches (misspellings, name variants, related entities), and previously waived conflicts.
4. Produces a markdown conflict-check memo a partner can review and sign in under 5 minutes.
5. Logs the run - timestamp, user, inputs, hits - in the firm's audit log so the documentation satisfies Rule 4-1.7(b).

### Why this matters in Jacksonville specifically

Jacksonville has a high density of multi-generational firms with 20-30 year matter databases. Pajcic & Pajcic alone has been running since 1974. When a partner at a 12-attorney firm opens a new auto-negligence matter and the adverse driver is a small business, the conflict surface is enormous - related LLCs, prior insurance carriers, business partners, family members. Manual checks miss things; the [American Bar Association cautions](https://www.americanbar.org/groups/professional_responsibility/publications/model_rules_of_professional_conduct/) that imputed conflicts under Rule 1.10 require comprehensive screening.

A Claude Code skill running a fuzzy match against the firm's full 50,000+ matter history returns better recall than the paralegal would, in under 60 seconds, and writes a clean memo. For a firm opening 25-50 new matters per month, that's 20-40 paralegal hours per month back - and matter-open time drops from 2-4 days to under 4 hours.

sections.heading: Agent 3: Document review and discovery summarization
sections.body:
Agent 3 is where the cost savings become material on the income statement. Jacksonville's litigation boutiques and the corporate practices feeding the insurance-and-banking economy (FIS, Black Knight, the homeowners-insurance bad-faith bar) spend serious associate hours on document review, deposition summary, contract redlining, and discovery production analysis. At $250-$400/hour associate rates, those hours add up fast.

A Claude-powered document review agent does three things very well:

- **Deposition summarization.** Takes a full transcript (or rough-of-the-rough), produces a structured summary indexed by topic, flags contradictions with prior testimony, and surfaces key admissions. Typically replaces 2-4 hours of associate time per deposition.
- **Contract redlining.** Compares a counterparty's proposed contract against the firm's playbook of preferred positions and produces a clean redline plus a deviation memo. Replaces 1-2 hours of associate time per contract - critical for the corporate transactional work Jacksonville firms do for FIS, Mayo Clinic, Florida Blue, and the JAXPORT logistics ecosystem.
- **Discovery review.** Processes large document productions, tags privileged and responsive material with confidence scores, and drafts a privilege log. Replaces hundreds of associate hours per significant litigation matter.

### Solving the hallucination problem

The legitimate fear is hallucination - Claude inventing facts that are not in the underlying document. By 2026 the fix is well-established: the agent runs in a retrieval-augmented configuration where every output is grounded in cited source passages from the actual documents. The final pass is a human associate spot-checking those citations. Reviews still drop 60-80% in time while consistency improves - the associate's job shifts from reading every page to verifying Claude's cited extracts, which is much higher leverage.

For insurance bad-faith cases (a huge Jacksonville segment given the 226% homeowners-premium spike), the document agent is especially powerful at flagging carrier conduct patterns across claim files, which is exactly the analysis that wins extracontractual damages.

sections.heading: Florida Bar Opinion 24-1 and compliance rules
sections.body:
Every Jacksonville firm we work with opens the engagement with the same question: "Is this even allowed under our ethics rules?" The answer is yes, and Florida is actually one of the most AI-friendly states for lawyers in the country. Here is the current picture.

### [Florida Bar Opinion 24-1](https://www.floridabar.org/etopinions/opinion-24-1/) - the foundational guidance

Adopted unanimously by the Florida Bar Board of Governors on January 19, 2024. It affirmatively permits use of generative AI in law practice and addresses four ethical areas:

1. **Confidentiality (Rule 4-1.6).** The lawyer must protect client information when using AI. Practically, this means using a deployment where the vendor does not train on inputs - Anthropic's commercial API meets this; consumer Claude.ai does not.
2. **Competence and accuracy (Rule 4-1.1).** Lawyers must supervise and verify AI outputs. AI is a tool; the lawyer is responsible for the work product.
3. **Fair billing (Rule 4-1.5).** If AI compresses 3 hours of work into 12 minutes, you bill 12 minutes - not 3 hours. The Bar specifically flags inflated billing as an Opinion 24-1 violation.
4. **Advertising and intake (Rule 4-7.13).** Chatbots must identify themselves as AI, not human staff. Chatbots must not deliver legal advice or negotiate claims. Lawyers should include screening questions for represented persons.

### Florida Rule 4-5.3 (supervising non-lawyer assistance)

The Bar treats AI as a non-lawyer assistant for supervision purposes. A designated attorney must supervise the deployment with documented review processes.

### Practical Florida compliance posture

The three-agent stack is fully deployable under current Florida rules when (a) the agents run on Anthropic's commercial Claude API with a DPA in place (and a BAA where PHI is involved), (b) the intake agent identifies itself as AI in its first message, (c) a designated partner supervises the deployment under Rule 4-5.3, and (d) every client-facing output and matter-opening decision is reviewed by a licensed attorney before being relied upon. None of this is theoretical - we have shipped this configuration at firms across Northeast Florida.

sections.heading: The 90-day Jacksonville deployment plan
sections.body:
Most Jacksonville firms want a phased rollout that proves ROI before committing budget for the full stack. Here is the 90-day plan we run.

### Days 1-15: Discovery and intake agent

- **Week 1: workflow mapping.** A working session with the firm's intake coordinator, an associate, and the managing partner. We map every step from first lead touch (Google Ads click, referral call, walk-in) to matter opening. We instrument the current speed-to-lead baseline.
- **Week 2: intake agent deployment to web chat only.** Soft launch with monitoring. Every transcript flows to a Slack channel where the intake partner reviews the first 30 conversations in real time.

### Days 16-30: Intake expansion + conflict-check skill

- **Week 3: intake agent expanded** to after-hours SMS and phone (using Vapi or Bland integrated with Claude for voice). For firms targeting Spanish-language intake (~5.8% of Jacksonville speaks Spanish at home), we enable language detection on first contact.
- **Week 4: Claude Code conflict-check skill** deployed against the firm's matter database. Two paralegals trained on the skill's output format and the new audit-log workflow.

### Days 31-60: Document review pilot

- **Weeks 5-6:** document review agent deployed on one active litigation matter - typically a Jacksonville auto-negligence case with substantial document production or an insurance bad-faith matter. Associates use it on deposition summary and discovery review.
- **Weeks 7-8:** expand to contract redlining for the corporate group (FIS-adjacent vendor contracts, JAXPORT logistics agreements, real estate transactional work).

### Days 61-90: Optimization and measurement

- **Weeks 9-10:** tune the intake agent's system prompt based on real Jacksonville transcripts. Many firms find their qualification standard needs tightening after 30 days - the agent was booking unqualified PI calls, for example, and the partner wants a higher threshold.
- **Weeks 11-12:** run the formal ROI report. Compare consultation booking rate, average matter-open time, and associate hours per matter against the day-zero baseline.

At day 90, the firm has a deployed, ethics-compliant, measurable AI stack and the data to keep it funded.

sections.heading: Real ROI benchmarks for Jacksonville firms
sections.body:
Numbers vary by practice area, but here is what we typically see at 4-25 attorney Jacksonville firms after 90 days of the full three-agent stack:

- **Intake**: 30-45% increase in consultation booking rate. Most of the gain comes from after-hours coverage, faster speed-to-lead, and bilingual capability - all three matter more in Jacksonville than in markets without the same demographics.
- **Conflict checks**: matter-open time drops from 2-4 days to under 4 hours. For high-volume PI practices opening 30-50 matters monthly, that's a meaningful working-capital improvement.
- **Document review**: 60-80% reduction in associate hours per matter on review-heavy tasks, with no observed accuracy degradation when used in retrieval-augmented mode.
- **Total monthly Claude/hosting cost**: $1,200-$2,500 depending on firm size and volume.
- **Typical payback period**: 4-6 weeks for the intake agent alone for most Jacksonville firms, less if you count after-hours answering-service replacement.

The one Jacksonville-specific number that consistently surprises managing partners: when bilingual intake is enabled, **Spanish-language leads convert at higher rates than English** because the firm captures a market the dominant competitors don't serve well. Morgan & Morgan does Spanish marketing, but small Jacksonville firms with Spanish-only intake forms historically miss leads they could have won. The Claude intake agent closes that gap on day one.

The other quietly large win: the intake agent typically captures 18-26% more *qualified* leads, not just more leads in absolute terms - the structured qualification questions get asked every time, with no fatigue and no skipping.

sections.heading: Common mistakes Jacksonville firms make
sections.body:
After working with Northeast Florida firms across PI, family law, immigration, and commercial practices, these are the patterns we see most often:

**Mistake 1: Buying a generic legal-AI SaaS instead of a custom Claude deployment.** Most off-the-shelf legal-AI products are tuned for the average national firm. Jacksonville is not average - the demographic mix, the insurance-bad-faith volume, the military-adjacent family law, and the Florida-specific ethics rules make a custom Claude deployment outperform generic vendors 2-to-3x in real measurement.

**Mistake 2: Ignoring Rule 4-7.13 disclosure on intake chatbots.** This is the single biggest Florida-specific ethics exposure. The chatbot must identify itself as AI. Firms that bolt a no-disclosure chatbot onto their website are inviting a Bar complaint.

**Mistake 3: Using Claude.ai consumer or ChatGPT free for client work.** Opinion 24-1's confidentiality requirement is not met by consumer chat products. Use the commercial Claude API tier with a DPA and (where PHI is involved) a BAA.

**Mistake 4: Overscoping the first deployment.** Firms that try to stand up all three agents in week one always fail. Start with intake, prove ROI, then expand to conflict-check and document review.

**Mistake 5: No designated AI-supervisor partner under Rule 4-5.3.** Designate a partner as the AI supervisor before turning anything on, and document their review cadence. This is one paragraph in your firm's policies manual - and it's the difference between defensible and indefensible if the Bar ever asks.

**Mistake 6: Not measuring.** If you can't show your managing partner the day-90 numbers - booking rate, matter-open time, associate hours - the budget gets cut. Instrument the baseline on day one.

sections.heading: How to pick an implementation partner
sections.body:
If you are a Jacksonville firm evaluating AI implementation partners in 2026, ask these five questions:

1. **Do you build on Anthropic's commercial Claude API specifically, with a DPA (and BAA where applicable) in place?** Anything else is an Opinion 24-1 confidentiality exposure.
2. **Can you produce Florida law firm case studies with named partners willing to take a reference call?** Generic AI consultancies cannot. Florida-specialized partners can.
3. **What is your deployment timeline?** A real partner deploys the first agent in 2-4 weeks. If they quote 6 months, they are upselling enterprise consulting.
4. **Who owns the system after deployment?** You should. Beware of lock-in models where the agent only runs on the vendor's proprietary platform - you want the firm to own the Claude API key, the system prompts, the integrations, and the audit logs.
5. **How do you handle Rule 4-5.3 supervision?** The answer should include a named partner relationship, a written supervision plan, and a documented monthly review process.

At manuv.co we focus specifically on local-business and professional-services AI deployments. We build custom Claude agents and Claude Code skills sized for firms in the 2-50 attorney range, with Florida Bar Opinion 24-1 compliance built into every deployment. If you want to map your Jacksonville firm's highest-ROI agent in a 30-minute call, the contact link is below.

faq.q: How much does it cost to deploy an AI agent at a Jacksonville law firm?
faq.a: Initial deployment of a Claude-powered intake agent for a typical 4-15 attorney Jacksonville firm runs $7,500-$16,000 one-time, with $400-$700/month ongoing for the Claude API, hosting, and SMS gateway. The full three-agent stack (intake + Claude Code conflict-check skill + document review) runs $18,000-$42,000 one-time and $1,200-$2,500/month ongoing. Payback for the intake agent alone is typically 4-6 weeks, faster if it displaces a paid after-hours answering service.

faq.q: Is using AI for client work allowed under Florida Bar ethics rules?
faq.a: Yes. Florida Bar Opinion 24-1 (January 19, 2024) affirmatively permits generative AI use in law practice subject to four guardrails: confidentiality under Rule 4-1.6, competence and verification under Rule 4-1.1, fair billing under Rule 4-1.5, and clear chatbot disclosure under Rule 4-7.13. A designated supervising partner under Rule 4-5.3 is also expected. Anthropic's commercial Claude API meets the confidentiality requirement; consumer Claude.ai and ChatGPT do not.

faq.q: What is the difference between Claude API and Claude.ai for a Florida law firm?
faq.a: Consumer Claude.ai is a chat interface for individual users. The commercial Claude API is the developer interface used by businesses - Anthropic does not train on commercial API inputs by default, and firms can sign a Data Processing Addendum and a Business Associate Agreement for the HIPAA-eligible Messages API. For client work under Florida Bar Opinion 24-1, only the commercial API is appropriate. The Workbench, Console, and Claude.ai consumer paths are not BAA-covered.

faq.q: How long does it take to deploy an AI intake agent at a Jacksonville firm?
faq.a: A focused deployment is 2-4 weeks for the intake agent alone. Week 1 is workflow mapping with the firm's intake coordinator and managing partner. Week 2 is build and soft-launch on the firm's website chat. Weeks 3-4 expand to after-hours SMS and phone with monitoring. The full three-agent stack (intake + conflict-check skill + document review) takes 90 days end-to-end with measured ROI reporting.

faq.q: Can AI agents handle Spanish-speaking clients in Jacksonville?
faq.a: Yes - and this is a real Jacksonville competitive advantage. Claude is natively multilingual and handles Spanish fluently. With 12.6% of Jacksonville residents being Hispanic and the Hispanic population growing 51% between 2010 and 2020, firms with Spanish-language intake capture leads competitors miss. We configure the agent to detect Spanish on first contact and respond in kind, which is especially impactful for immigration, personal-injury, and family-law practices on the Westside and in the Mandarin and Arlington corridors.

faq.q: Does the Florida Bar require the chatbot to disclose it is AI?
faq.a: Yes. Opinion 24-1 is explicit: a lawyer using an AI chatbot must inform prospective clients that they are communicating with an AI program and not with a lawyer or law firm employee. This must be conveyed in the first message and any reasonable user must be able to identify it. Failure to disclose is an Opinion 24-1 violation and implicates Rule 4-7.13's prohibition on deceptive advertising. We hard-code the disclosure into the system prompt and the opening web-chat greeting.

faq.q: What happens if the AI agent makes a mistake in client communication?
faq.a: Two safeguards apply. First, the intake agent's system prompt restricts it to triage and scheduling and explicitly refuses to provide legal advice or negotiate claims - so the surface area for harmful error is small. Second, a designated supervising partner reviews transcripts on a documented cadence under Rule 4-5.3. If a mistake occurs, the attorney-of-record is responsible under Opinion 24-1 and corrects it directly with the client. In our deployment history across Florida firms, the misclassifications we see are around over-conservative lead rejection, not improper advice.

faq.q: Will my paralegals or intake staff lose their jobs?
faq.a: In our Jacksonville deployments to date, no staff have been let go. The intake agent absorbs after-hours work that previously went to a paid answering service. The conflict-check skill returns 15-40 paralegal hours per month that get redirected to higher-value work like case preparation and client communication. The document review agent shifts associate time from reading to validating - the same associates handle more matters with no quality drop. Firms generally grow into the recovered capacity.

faq.q: How does the AI intake agent integrate with Clio, MyCase, or PracticePanther?
faq.a: All three platforms have public APIs that the agent uses to create contacts, draft matter records, attach intake notes, and schedule consultations. We have deployed against Clio Grow, Clio Manage, MyCase, PracticePanther, Smokeball, Filevine, and NetDocuments. Typical integration takes 2-3 days inside the 2-week intake-agent deployment window. For firms on legacy on-premises systems (more common in older Jacksonville firms), we deploy the Claude Code skill locally so data does not leave the firm's network.

faq.q: What about HIPAA - we represent medical malpractice plaintiffs sometimes?
faq.a: Anthropic offers a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) for the HIPAA-eligible Messages API path. If your Jacksonville firm handles PHI in intake or matter work (common in plaintiff-side medical malpractice and personal-injury practices), we configure the deployment under the BAA and add HIPAA-specific data-handling controls. Workbench, Console, and consumer Claude.ai are not BAA-covered, so all PHI-touching flows route through the API.

faq.q: Can I see this working at another Jacksonville or Florida law firm before I commit?
faq.a: Yes. We have client firms in Jacksonville, the Beaches, St. Johns County, and Tampa willing to take reference calls with prospective clients. Reach out through the contact link and we will arrange an introduction. Reference calls typically run 20-30 minutes and cover the deployment timeline, ROI measurement, and Florida ethics-compliance posture in the partner's own words.

faq.q: What if our matter management system is on-premises rather than cloud?
faq.a: We deploy the Claude Code conflict-check skill locally so it can query an on-premises SQL Server, NetDocuments instance, or legacy document store without data leaving the firm's network. The intake agent can also run in a hybrid pattern where conversation transcripts are summarized in the cloud and the final structured note is written into the on-premises system. Older Jacksonville mid-size firms running legacy infrastructure are fully supported.

faq.q: How do I get started with manuv.co?
faq.a: Book a 30-minute strategy call at [manuv.co/contact](/contact). We will walk through your firm's current intake and matter-open workflow, identify which of the three agents will return the most value first under Florida Bar Opinion 24-1, and provide a concrete deployment plan and quote. There is no cost for the assessment call, and you leave with an actionable plan whether or not you engage us.

internalLinks.text: AI Customer Support Agent (adaptable to legal intake)
